title: Interacting Brownian motions in the Kadar-Parisi-Zhang universality class
speaker: Herbert Spohn
affiliation: Technische Universitaet Muenchen; Member, School of Mathematics
abstract: A widely studied model from statistical physics consists of many (one-dimensional) Brownian motions interacting through a pair potential. The large scale behavior of this model has has been investigated by Varadhan, Yau, and others in the 90's. As a crucial property the model satisfies time-reversibility (alias detailed balance). Once this symmetry is broken, generically one crosses into the KPZ class. Two specific examples will be discussed.

proof_stages.label: Mesoscopic Ginzburg-Landau motivation from anharmonic chains
proof_stages.summary: The talk motivates replacing intractable microscopic Hamiltonian dynamics by stochastic mesoscopic Ginzburg-Landau models that retain the relevant symmetries, conservation laws, and finite-temperature fluctuations, with FPU chains and simulations as the guiding case.

proof_stages.label: Scalar reversible stochastic interface model
proof_stages.summary: A one-component conserved height/slope diffusion with shift symmetry is defined, its product invariant slope measures are identified, and known hydrodynamic and Gaussian t^{1/4} fluctuation results in the reversible case are recalled.

proof_stages.label: Asymmetric perturbation and KPZ scaling prediction
proof_stages.summary: Time-reversal symmetry is broken by asymmetric nearest-neighbor weights p and q while preserving the invariant measure, leading macroscopically to a nonlinear hyperbolic conservation law and mesoscopically to the expected KPZ t^{1/3} non-Gaussian fluctuations.

proof_stages.label: Integrable Toda realization with step data
proof_stages.summary: Because the general asymmetric equations are out of reach, the discussion restricts to solvable potentials; for the totally asymmetric Toda system with step initial data, the hydrodynamic law of large numbers and t^{1/3} GUE Tracy-Widom fluctuations are stated.

proof_stages.label: Point-interaction reflected Brownian motions
proof_stages.summary: A steep repulsive potential is replaced by boundary reflection in a Weyl chamber, and the totally asymmetric one-sided reflected Brownian system is shown, for flat initial spacing, to have t^{1/3} GOE Tracy-Widom fluctuations.

proof_stages.label: Stationary two-point KPZ scaling and simulation comparison
proof_stages.summary: For stationary exponential gaps, the two-point slope correlation is predicted to have the universal KPZ scaling function f_KPZ, whose Fourier transform is the curve compared with the earlier FPU simulation, while different initial data are noted to yield different universal laws.

proof_stages.label: Partial asymmetry through oblique reflection and duality
proof_stages.summary: The point-interaction generator is extended to intermediate asymmetry by oblique boundary reflection, and a p/q-swapped dual process with an explicit duality function is introduced as the main tool for reducing infinite-particle expectations to tractable few-particle computations, followed by outlook toward vector-valued and nonintegrable models.

examples.name: Fermi-Pasta-Ulam chain as microscopic motivation
examples.note: The FPU Hamiltonian chain is used to extract the modeling rules that a mesoscopic stochastic model should preserve, especially shift symmetry, finite-temperature fluctuations, and the conserved elongation, momentum, and energy fields.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker writes the FPU Hamiltonian, derives Newton/discrete wave equations, and checks shift symmetry and conservation laws for the chain

examples.name: Lepri FPU structure-factor simulation
examples.note: A numerical simulation of a 4000-site FPU chain is compared with a KPZ/Ginzburg-Landau scaling curve to illustrate the predictive value of the stochastic model rather than to prove the theorem.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker describes Lepri’s simulation parameters, defines the elongation correlation and its Fourier transform S(q,omega), and interprets the scaling plot against the theory curve
